sentences.text: At that time, so-called children from mixed marriages and partners, mixed marriages were released.
Actually, there were two types of mixed marriages.
One was the so-called mixed marriage.
It had one partner Jewish, the other partner not Jewish.
Those were the regular mixed marriages.
And then they had the so-called privileged mixed marriages.
They had two wed partners, one not a Jew, one a Jew, but the children were raised as non-Jews.
They didn't have to wear the star, neither had the Jewish partner to wear the star.
The regular marriages were either without children or children that they raised Jewish.
In that case, the children had to wear the star and the Jewish partner had to wear the star.
And those children who were raised Jewish were called Geltungsjuden, meaning they were considered Jews.
They had to wear the star and actually had to do the forced labor.
That is a main difference.
I was a so-called Geltungsjuden, had to wear the star.
No, my mother did not.
My father had to, but not my mother.
Had I not been raised Jewish, none of us would have to wear the star.
In my case, it was known.
My father had been active in the Jewish congregation.
We went to the synagogue.
How could we have said that was not the case.
Yes, it was tried.
I know for a fact that some people tried to get out of it.
In fact, even at some times, women claimed that their husband wasn't actually the father of the child, that the father was some Aryan.
That was tried, too.
And it was tried too-- a case actually of a school-- school friend-- went to the same class.
And we did the forced labor together, and he actually one day disappeared.
Went to the organization and talked-- this was a labor organization, where they could have-- Mischlings were allowed.
Lo and behold, he didn't wear the star anymore.
And actually, in effect, reverted from Geltungsjuden to Mischling.
I'm quite sure that the mother, who was well to do, bribed someone to do this.
This was well known that you could do this.
You found the right person who's changing some documents, that was done.
In this one case, I know for sure.
But why not?
Did you know of any incidents where a mixed marriage was dissolved, where the non-Jew was persuaded to divorce the-- the Jew?
Or--
Yes, I know in one case, a regular marriage, no children, the husband was somewhat-- I would almost say probably somewhat mentally handicapped.
Anyhow, apparently the woman, the wife, wanted to divorce him.
And my mother actually went and told her that you can't do this.
If you divorce your husband, he will be deported.
I don't-- I don't believe it was successful.
They actually had the divorce, and he was deported.
In another case, the wife waited until the end of the war, and then there was.
Yes.
In fact, I'll give you the name of where the-- the wife waited until the war was over.
It was our physician, Dr.
Frankel.
Absolutely, he said-- when the care packages came, he saw to it that she got some care packages.
You saved my life.
They lived themselves apart, as he put it.
But he still didn't want a divorce.
At the moment the war was over, the divorce, yes.
Absolutely.
If the-- if the non-Jewish partner died or divorced, the moment that happened, deportation.
The so-called Mischling, the person-- the child that was not raised Jewish and didn't have to wear the star.
Yeah, there was second degree, but actually, the-- they-- they didn't deport, or they were actually not involved.
They couldn't serve at certain-- I don't think they took into the army, and they couldn't do this or couldn't do that.
But there was no deportation.
They didn't have to wear the star, and they got regular ration cards and so on.
The important point was whether the child of a mixed marriage was raised Jewish or not.
That determined it.
Later on, yes.
I think towards the end of the war, they had to do forced labor too.
No, probably not.
At that time, granted, they took all-- the work force was low, and they took women too.
But to the best of my knowledge, my mother had to do the forced labor because she was married to a Jew.
I believe she worked at a-- uniform, making uniforms.
Yes.
He actually first, he worked in a factory, and-- and later on he had to clean up after air raids.
And I had to do the same.
Actually, after we went to the Grosse Hamburger Strasse, Mr.
Bruner asked me what are you doing right now.
I told him I work at the social service agency.
He goes, no, no.
That-- starting tomorrow, you report to the labor exchange and you work elsewhere here.
Do something really-- some work that has some value for the war.
And so I did and I was-- but then they send me to a factory that made the best [?
I can. ?] Of course, you never really knew what you were doing.
But I believe we did certain materials for balloons, for meteorologic balloons.
I know because there was one section where they did meteorology and balloons.
And I'm quite sure what we did, there.
What we did many of, again and again.
But of course, I kept my eyes and ears open and I'm quite sure for those weather balloons what they were, that's what we did.
Not to my knowledge.
At that time, look, you had to-- sabotage probably was possible in assembly.
If you had to turn out a little piece of metal, yeah long, you couldn't sabotage very much.
Either you did it or you didn't do it.
And the moment you didn't do it, then immediately it was obvious.
There was hardly any-- we have to sabotage.
Yes, that [INAUDIBLE] worked as slowly as we could.
But then, on the other hand, the people were quite decent.
And had we not reached our norm, they would have been in trouble, and you know what would happen.
No, we did what we were supposed to do and that was it.
Privately held enterprises that did war work.
They were all Jewish.
Women and I believe I and another-- actually what happened, my classmate from high school was there too.
For some reason, by pure coincidence-- in fact, he was already there.
And I came afterwards.
Good luck.
No, we brought our own stuff, what we had.
Yes, we were paid.
We got paid.
Of course, what happened in my father's case, all the money wasn't given to us, but had to be put in a special bank account.
And my father was only allowed to take out so and so much.
Only for a medical and dental bills was a special distribution, you could get more.
Exactly, and if for some reason we didn't-- why did that happen in my case, I believe, at one time one of my father's relatives left my mother some money.
It was actually a pitiful sum, but it went over a certain amount.
And immediately that happened, the-- the bank account couldn't be accessed freely anymore.
But it didn't actually-- I know I got a kind of pay slip, and I knew that the money was put in the bank directly.
I don't know.
I doubt it.
[INAUDIBLE] In our case, my father didn't have any.
And actually, we didn't.
Probably what happened, we had a friend too, but of course, the property was owned now by the non-Jewish partner.
But honestly, I don't know.
Yes, much more.
Because what happened, that once we worked-- shortly after I left the factory, [? if we can ?] go back to it, there was another regulation.
You had to have special passes to use public transportation.
We had to have it.
My mother, of course, didn't.
Yes, absolutely.
Even I came along.
Even after the star, I [? didn't have ?] the star.
Probably not officially, but I was really careful.
What did you do if your neighbor reported you to the local political boss there.
You realize in Germany each precinct has a political-- what's called a political boss.
A kind of-- they called it [? chrisligter ?], that took care of the political aspect of snooping around.
This you had to have some courage to do that, you see.
Yes.
Actually, the moment my father lost his job, my aunt, my mother's sister, took care of me.
Absolutely.
From socks to hat, everything.
Her name was Elfride.
I called her Aunt [?
Fridel. ?] It was-- actually, we met at night and she gave me food packages.
And I remember we met one night, it was pretty dark, side street, I didn't have a star.
Lo and behold, we walked together.
Who shows up around the corner, the big Nazi of the neighborhood.
But my aunt rose to the occasion.
Ah, good evening, Frau what have you, I forgot the name.
How are you?
Oh, yeah, he's just helping me carrying my-- I remember distinctly.
So she gave some sort of justification for your being there.
So what am I going to do.
Packages, I am helping.
What could you say?
She was absolutely-- regularly we received food packages.
And even my mother met with her.
But that time, for some reason, I had to do it.
I don't know what happened.
But anyhow, yes they did.
Dressler.
D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R.
Her husband was director of the equivalent of General Electric.
He actually was a party member, but still as you see, he did everything to aid and abet his wife until he couldn't.
But he was fairly high up and had some connections where he could get some extra food and help.
Furthermore, they had two country houses, probably the equivalent of [INAUDIBLE].
One in east Berlin, west Berlin.
Some of our valuables we stored there.
And strangely enough, the east one with the Russians survived.
And the other one, well, the Russians came there too, but we found out later on, the Russians didn't take anything.
The people who were the landlords of this, they just took it, they claimed that the Russians had done it.
But, be that as it may.
Oh, certainly, yes.
If they hadn't been fast on their feet, the [INAUDIBLE] what's it doing here?
Yes.
It was Ernst Dressler.
Ernst, yeah, Ernst.
And actually, in the beginning he took me out Sundays, in the mornings.
He had a car, even before Hitler.
And early.
And we went out to the suburbs, swimming.
And he did an awful lot for me.
Not everyone that was a party member was an outright Nazi.
Yes, sometimes he and my father argued about the Jews, but he still was very helpful.
Well, what usually happened, comes at night, you looked to the sky.
Well, it's going be cloudy, we're going to have a quiet night.
If there's stars, we're going to have an air raid.
And that got worse and worse.
In the beginning, only the British during the night.
Then the Americans during the day.
It was primarily for hours you sat down.
Absolutely.
And of course what happened, in apartment houses they kept the air raid shelters separate.
The Jews could not sit with the other ones, you see, it was quite--.
And one time, in our first apartment, we had to go out on the street to go to the next house, because there was no Jewish-- we were the only Jews in the building.
And the next house had several.
They had set aside the shelter for the Jews.
[INAUDIBLE] Jews either, but they kept us separate.
And there you're sitting and waiting.
What would you do?
One time, you'd say are they going to get hit and then again, you felt well, tonight we are really going to get it.
Once we were sitting and school was still on, it was after 2 o'clock, the school was late one hour and-- If you got released from the air raid shelter before 2:00?
If the all clear came after 2 o'clock, we went to school one and a half hour later.
If it came before, we had to show up at the regular time.
Now, can you imagine us young people, oh, if it has-- it's quiet now, now we need two more minutes.
If the all clear doesn't come now.
Sometimes happened, sometimes not.
I guess school boys are school boys, no matter what.
Absolutely.
I wasn't any different.
Hooray, we made it [INAUDIBLE].
And of course, when that happened, they had to make up the time.
Normally, a class was 45 minutes, or 50 minutes, I don't quite recall.
After the all clear came after 2 o'clock, then all class meetings were 30 minutes, you see.
And of course, then sometimes you didn't have to account for your homework.
For some reason we took advantage of this.
This was approximately '42.
I think '43. '43-- at that time, I worked at that factory.
I remember it was '43, I'm quite sure.
Oh, yes.
I had a pretty good idea.
Well, you could read the papers, headlines, radio.
We listened to the radio, and suddenly, tatatarata, fanfares, drums, a new report from the front.
We did this, we did that.
We just occupied this, and so on.
But you could tell, at the beginning there were one probably every couple of hours.
Later on, it was a couple of days, and then none.
And obviously, you could tell.
Oh yes, yes.
You could see, read lines.
Don't forget, we had the map, and you could tell.
And people came back to-- from the front and they told that things were not going as well anymore.
And let's face it, [INAUDIBLE], they had memorial services, closed down on movie theaters afterwards.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, reduced proportionally.
Absolutely.
I remember distinctly the first-- it was '39, the Christmas '39-- oh, boy, all the French cognac and what have you-- or was it '40 I don't-- one of the earliest one there.
But of course, that stopped, quite a bit, I assure you.
Well, actually, we told the latest jokes, jokes which were circulated not by the Jews, but by the Germans.
Actually they had quite a bit of a sense of humor.
Rations-- well, when will the war be over?
Oh, quite easy.
The war will be over when the British eat rats, and we in Germany eat rats [GERMAN].
See, stuff like this.
And I must tell you-- particularly in Berlin, Berlin had very good, very sense of humor.
Italy, when they capitulated, then the definition, what is one Italian?
A tenor.
Two Italians?
A gang of thieves.
Three Italians?
A military retreat.
See, stuff like this.
We even made fun of the [? hospital ?] song.
[INAUDIBLE].
Now, could this be dangerous?
Could there be repercussions?
Of course.
Of course, it's dangerous.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
You were very careful.
No, not to my knowledge.
I just remember those.
No, actually probably had-- I don't recall.
Those just came to mind, those jokes we picked up.
But otherwise, were at the factory, we didn't-- we were so busy, we didn't talk to--
Yes.
It was absolutely amazing.
They could be bombed out, elevated subway, street cars.
We had them within hours, it was running again.
It was amazing how they could do it.
Well, there was German efficiency.
They were very efficient.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
Actually, the-- unless we got direct hits to the factory, not too much time was left therefore-- I remember the air raid came during the day, we knew that the-- the elevated had been hit.
By the time 5, 6 o'clock, we went down, it was already fixed.
Yes, perhaps only on one line, and we had to wait a little bit, and it was-- service was irregular.
But they had service.
Yes, but a special pass.
A Jew.
Yes.
Well, to the Jew or the Jew of so and so.
And they got permission to use whatever it was, subway.
In my case, all public transportation conveniences.
Absolutely.
That was-- there was a law, no Jew were permitted to use public transportation.
Frankly, when there was seats, we sat down.
Rather not, of course, did-- if it was full.
And of course, if there was a big air raid and you had to go to work, and people tried to get on the train, the train coming already filled, I have to get on, I have to go to work.
You'd rather not try to get in there with your star.
Later, I was very lucky with the star.
Whenever you took off the star and they caught you, you were in bad trouble.
Oh, yes.
I did it.
What happened once, I was stopped twice.
Once by a plainclothes man.
He just wondered what is a young fellow doing out of uniform.
OK, and I showed him my papers, which exclude me from the army.
He let me go.
And a second time, I was stopped I think it was a Gestapo.
He showed me his badge, Gestapo.
He saw my-- he knew darned well that I didn't wear the star.
He looked at me-- go.
He could have arrested me.
Actually, one of my former classmates got arrested.
They would have put me in the camp.
I hoped to get me out.
Oh, who knows what.
Deportation-- you don't know.
You'd rather not find out.
He got out.
They got him-- actually the-- the boss for the working crew actually went-- [INAUDIBLE] that he was absolutely needed.
They couldn't do the demolition without him.
He was an expert in what have you.
He got out.
He was very lucky, because that particular boss who did it was not very nice to my father.
But since he did that, they overlooked it and nothing happened.
I wonder what he would have done otherwise.
But OK, it happened, twice.
I was exceedingly fortunate whatever happened.
For instance, when I left the factory one day-- at that time, there were no more lists.
They just grabbed people when they probably needed to.
I don't know how many Jews.
They came around.
The SS came around in trucks, collected everyone at the various factories and transported us to another transition camps.
Crew, Crew was a nightclub.
These huge halls.
And they took out the chairs, tables, and used it as collection.
And there, they brought everyone in, regardless who it was.
And then, they more or less weeded people out.
Let's see who we have got here.
Lo and behold, they got-- it was a friend, Gerhardt, we worked together in the factory.
And they realized we were both Geltungsjuden.
And the guy was quite nice.
And said OK, good luck.
I don't want to see you anymore.
Bye-bye, and let us go.
Of course, we knew exactly what would happen if we would have stayed there.
And there again, bureaucracy.
Bureaucracy dictated that children of some people, mixed marriage, either adults or their non-Jewish partner, or children from a mixed marriage, were not to be deported at the time.
Here I am.
And very often in other countries, or away from Berlin, probably that wouldn't have happened.
It was much too cumbersome.
Take him.
Otherwise, I wouldn't be here.
You must say German bureaucracy helped to save my life.
